In these cases, we  use past tenses and would to 'distance' our things that will probably not happen, situations that are untrue or imaginary, We   use special structures with  when we are talking about unreal situations - 1  unreal     situations What would you do if you lost your job?

239 special     structures       with    past    tenses For if + will in reported speech (e.g. 1 don’t know if I’ll be ready), ►    260.3.

For if + will (e.g. if it will make you feel better), ►    243.

As soon as we have fine weather, I'm going to paint the windows.

- If we have fine weather tomorrow, I'm going to paint the windows.

I'll give her your love when I see her. (     .. .-when-4-will see her.) not - I'll give her your love if I see her. (     ... if J will see her.) This happens after most conjunctions (►   231). Compare: In an t/-dause, we  normally use a present tense to talk about the future.

2 present      tense     with    future     meaning Because you didn't study physics at school, you won't understand this book.

- If you didn't study physics at school, you won't understand this book.

As Jack didn't come to work yesterday, he was probably ill.

- If Jack didn't come to work yesterday, he was probably ill.

Iron goes red when it gets very hot.

- Oil floats if you pour it on water.

present, past tenses to the past, and so on. Compare: tenses with if as with other conjunctions. (less direct, more polite) - Will it be all right if I bring a friend? (direct request) If I won this race, I'd... (said by the slowest runner) - If I win this race, I'll... (said by the fastest runner) If I became President, I'd... (said by a schoolboy) - If! become President, I'll. .. (said by a candidate in an election) that a future situation is impossible, imaginary or less probable. Compare: difference of time.
